w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Machinery rewrote the social and natural worlds in the nineteenth century, and the interaction between literature and technology was correspondingly wide and deep. The landscape of Great Britain was transformed as the practical application of knowledge in new and improved technologies created the most industrialized country in the world. William Blake, on the cusp of the period, summarized the transition in the contrast between “green and pleasant lands” and “dark satanic mills” (“Jerusalem
” 1808). Indeed, the widespread adoption of coal to heat homes and
, trains, and ships through the steam engine
, coupled with the shift of populations to industrial and metropolitan cent

s and the later introduction of gas and electricity to the urban scene, produced by the end of the century a world that would have been virtually unrecognizable at its start. The new environmental phenomenon of air pollution led to new modes of perception evident in the murky London nights of Oscar Wilde’s Dorian Gray
Stevenson’s Mr. Hyde, and Arthur Conan Doyle’s Sherlock Holmes, as well as
mpressionist movement in painting. The literature of Victorian Britain
Victorians and the Machine: The Literary Response to Technology

is incomprehensible without a sense of the myriad ways in which technology continuously reshaped, even as it was itself significantly shaped by, the material, social, aesthetic, and intellectual world.

Victorian technological developments are deeply intertwined with scientific advancements and the flowering of an industrialized and globalized economy. Britain’s military might and continual imperial expansion relied heavily upon
the manufacturing industry with raw materials from the colonies. Literature chronicled the
transformation in daily lives. In 1832, a person could travel to the coast at a rate of roughly 100 miles per day in a coach pulled by horses
and then cross the Atlantic by sail in about three weeks, in clothing and with personal ef
fects made by hand. Mail travel
ed at the same speed. By the time of Victoria’s death in 1901, mundane experience incorporated a panoply of inventions that included the steamship, omnibus, tram, and subway,
the launch of the automobile, the use of chloroform for medical anesthesia
and the invention of X-rays, the telegraph and the telephone, gas and electric lighting

, and the industrialization of the production of everything from textiles to mechanical pencils. One could cross the Atlantic by large steamship in six days, purchase ready-to-wear clothing, enjoy the affordable luxury of safety matches, pens, pins, toys, and playing cards, and read newspapers, books

and magazines produced on an unprecedented scale. Significant developments in printing technologies presaged an explosion of new media and the increasing accessibility and prominence of images, from still photography early in the period to recorded sound and moving pictures by its end.
The most direct impact of technologies on the production of literature was the increasing scale of publication enabled by steam-powered printing from 1814,

stereotype or cliché printing from 1827, and new paper technologies (mechanized from 1803, with wood pulp from 1844) which allowed for economies of scale in the production of literature and print more generally. Periodicals
that followed the repeal of the stamp tax.
a print-based mass culture even when most people were still illiterate (Anderson 1991).
rich interplay between artists and authors in many genres, and periodicals such as the
achieved estimated publics of over a million. Rising literacy led to the
writers such as Ouida or Marie Corelli sold hundreds of thousands of
runs of 500 or 1000 copies sold primarily to lending libraries. Short fiction flourished because it was conducive to inclusion in periodicals, in which form most literature was read, and
. Authors often got their start with stories, whether George Eliot in high-brow quarterlies such as
, or writers such as Amelia B. Edwards and Eliza Meteyard in more humble weeklies such as those published by Dickens
. The period thus saw substantial development of the short story by authors including Elizabeth Gaskell and Margaret Oliphant with their domestic histories, Dickens with
and other ghost stories, Sheridan LeFanu with his gothic tales, Arthur Conan Doyle, with
, and New Woman writers such as George Egerton and Vic
The vibrancy of print culture is reflected in influential social critic Thomas Carlyle’s celebration in
Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History
” as a product of the press: “so long as the wondrous art of
, or of Ready-writing which we call

, subsists, he may be expected to continue, as one of the main forms of Heroism for all future ages.” Writers are producers of intellectual property with “copy-rights and copy-wrongs.” Indeed, just as patent law fostered a culture of technological invention and investment, the extension of intellectual pro
perty law advocated by authors including Dickens
positioned writers as entrepreneurs, and limited liability, introduced in 1855, enabled printing and publishing ventures.
Capitalist ideology was embodied most directly in the literature of the self-made man, in exemplary biographies such as those of Samuel Smiles’
which idealized the “genius” of inventors and industrial entrepreneurs within the ideological framework of liberalism and laissez-faire economics.

(1856), the most prominent fictional example, represented her hero’s rise from orphanhood to economic pillar as a consequence of his self-reliance, work ethic, model Christianity, and embrace of new technologies such as steam power and vaccination.

Carlyle’s “Signs of the Times” (1840) proclaimed “the Age of Machinery in every outward and inward sense of that word,” but writers were generally uninterested in details. Tennyson’s invocation of the early railway in “Locksley Hall” (1842) prophetically yokes a Victorian sense of the inevitability of progress to this revolutionary new form of transportation while getting the specifics wrong:
Let the great world spin for ever down the ringing grooves of change.
Victorians were understandably fascinated and repelled by the technology that transformed travel, industry, commerce, and communications. George Eliot’s novel
72) weaves class-based controversy over the railway into her tapestry of a market town struggling with reform in the 1830s.
The railway was an often fatal harbinger of change

a railway boom, the treacherous manager James Carker becomes the first literary character killed by a train, echoing the actual fate of Member of Parliament William Huskisson at the landmark opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway in 1830. Elizabeth Gaskell conjoins the railway and serial publication to signal the incursion of the new industrializing world into the sleepy village of
epresentatives of the old and the new
the younger Captain Brown that it is “vulgar, and below the dignity of literature, to publish in numbers.” Brown dies by train as a consequence of absorption in the latest number of the serialized
37). The association is ironically apt, since the railway was the backbone of the new distribution system.
Trains also contributed to fictional technique, providing novel plot devices, particularly within the new form of sensation fiction. Mary Elizabeth Braddon’s

(1862) juxtaposes an old-fashioned one-handed clock on the tower of a landed estate with the modern temporality adopted by barrister-turned-detective Robert Audley. To surveille and expose the eponymous

Audley must operate on “railway time,” which signified both the synchronization or standardization of time via telegraph across disparate localities initiated by the Great Western Railway in 1840 and a quicker pace of life. This shift paralleled developments in the factory towns, where workers’ lives were regulated not by the older rhythms of the natural world and agricultural life, but by factory bells and the demands of large-scale mechanized industrial production.

The temporal rhythms of machine production found their way into literature. Thomas Hood’s “The Song of the Shirt” (1843) describes the hand sewing of an exploited seamstress doing piece-work, but the repetitive cadences of the refrains “Stitch! Stitch! Stitch!” and “Work – work – work” invoke the alienation and relentless rhythms of industrial factory work, although the sewing machine had yet to come into widespread use. The pulses of electricity, including telegraphic ones, ran through the “physiological poetics” of poetesses, Spasmodics, and of later poets such as Hopkins (Rudy 2009).
The production and consumption of literature itself were shaped by these new rhythms (Zemka 2012).
the industrial model of externally and technologically regulated labour. His

(1883) records his practice of writing for three hours from 5:30 each morning, exacting 250 words every quarter of an hour to produce “as regularly as my watch went” a minimum of 2500 words before breakfast. This “division of time,” he argued, produced three 3-volume novels in 10 months, a claim buttressed by his immense productivity. Trollope also embodied the close connection between literature and the transportation system, since as Inspector for

the Post Office, he travelled the country by train, writing as he went. Time was externally regulated for writers who produced for the periodical press or in serial form, and many felt severely pressed by deadlines. Dickens, who launched the novel in parts as a profitable technology of literary production, famously missed only one publishing deadline in his life, but many including Wilkie Collins, who exploited the form’s potential for suspense in highly original ways, found it an enormous strain. Serialized novels and other periodicals punctuated and provided a rhythm to the lives of their readers that internalized patterns of consumerism. They launched the parallel tracks of cultural and everyday experience that characterize a technologized entertainment industry, intensifying the impact of popular literary texts and allowing for commodification of that success. The Trilby hat, named for the heroine of George du Maurier’s novel (1894)
is a famous example of literary merchandizing; earlier ones include Pickwick-themed china.

: advertisements bundled with serial parts or embedded in periodicals supplemented income from purchasers, introducing the now common model of commodifying the consumer to fund cultural production (Brake 2001).
the practice of selling cheap fiction in railway stalls for consumption during journeys and also
signature suspense and shocking plots developed by authors such as Braddon and Col
Kipling’s story “Mrs. Bathurst” (1904) explores the unsettling effects of late-century cinematographs or moving pictures,
technologies of the moving image, including the kaleidoscope and the magic lantern,
the relationships amongst mobility, bodies, and reading processes that so disturbed critics of sensation novel
The dominant Victorian literary mode of realism
telegraph, which provided near-instantaneous communication from the 1830s, and
As established by Laura Otis in
Networking: Communicating with Bodies and Machines in the Nineteenth Century
insight into emerging understandings of the implications
new media in a culture increasingly defined
by the circulation of information via networks (
Fiction in the Age of Photography

and improved markedly over the course of the century, not only governs the intelligibility of fiction, but also ushers in a link between mass visuality and the formation of identity itself. The boom in plate-glass manufacturing by mid-century, coupled with improvements in steel technologies, made possible both large shop windows and the construction of the Crystal Palace that housed the Great Exhibition of 1851, a showcase of Britain’s industrial and imperial might.
shift to a culture of display and consumption in the exploration of verisimilitude, commodification, and fetishization in Victorian fiction.
New theatre technologies have been blamed for the emphasis on spectacle that

drove “serious” or “high” drama into other genres, such as the dramatic monologue and the highly dialogized form of Victorian fiction, until the late Victorian period. Improved building technologies and lighting expanded theatres
elodramatic plots were heightened by improved stage machinery
a life-size version of a locomotive onto the stage to
degree of material realism to the
last-minute rescue of a body on the tracks; other productions involved ships and live horses
, or exploited gadgets such as magic lanterns
in Victorian literary engagement with technology, but
or instance, in fiction photography often aligns with the manipulated, the unreal, and the abstract (Novak 2008). N
persistently linked to the boundaries of the knowable
and the limits of natural law. I
the apparent alignment of communication technologies and pseudo-sciences such as mesmerism and spiritualism underpins both psychoanalysis and literary modernism.

Text technologies surface repeatedly as a preoccupation of Victorian writers, who probe the implications of new media and modes of representation with uncanny insight. In the story “The Signal-Man” (1866), Dickens
the telegraph with an overwhelming sense of dread associated with rail accidents. His eponymous

sees a spectre, announced by the electric bell, that presages first a major accident, next the death of a young female passenger, and finally his own death by train. However, the middle-class narrator leaves us unsure whether the tragedy is of supernatural or psychological origin, given his insight that the signal-man is a new kind of labourer, an isolated knowledge worker haunted by the immense responsibility of ensuring the safety of passengers by correctly interpreting signals sent along wires. Rudyard Kipling’s later story “Wireless” (1902) offers less scope for rationalizing. It juxtaposes an early attempt to intercept a radio signal from a ship with the utterance of extensive fragments of the poetry of John Keats by an inadvertently intoxicated, tubercular young pharmacist whose circumstances are similar to
the Romantic poet, of whose work he is ignorant.
he narrator tries to rationalize it,
is left uneasy by this uncanny
boundaries between humans and machines, from the metonymic use of the terms “hands” or “mechanics” to refer to factory workers to the personification of various forms of technology.
he rise of self-directing, tireless, independently powered machines not only led to social conflict but to cultural anxiety about the boundary between human

. Human labour and human agency seemed equally threatened by the design and partial implementation by mathematician Charles Babbage of a “Difference Engine” that could emulate human mental processes of calculation. Babbage’s next design, the “Analytical Engine,” was never built, but, in extrapolating from the use of punch cards to govern the complex patterns produced by Jacquard weaving looms, it anticipated the programmable computer. Andrew Ure’s
” living machines “apparently instinct with the thought, feeling, and tact of
the description of the initial trial of the steam-engine in Craik’s
likens the piston-rod to “the strong ri
ght arm of some automaton giant

the machine starts up, “Of a sudden a soul had been put into that wonderful creature of man's making, that inert mass of wood and metal, mysteriously combined. The monster was alive.” Such monsters, allied as they are
brought power and prosperity to the middle classes, are generally benign in Victorian fiction. The automata or robots in
(1871), in which Edward Bulwer-Lytton imagines an alternative, egalitarian society by extrapolating from existing technology
offer a striking contrast to the animated corpse of the monster in Mary Shelley’s

The intersection of the natural body with Victorian technologies is nevertheless often fraught. George Eliot’s novella “The Lifted Veil” (1859) plays with precognition and clairvoyance in exploring the intersection of the material or physiological with the spiritual or psychological; the story culminates in the use of transfusion
fuel for reflection on processes of perception
the microscope, telescope, and stethoscope. The potential for abuse of medical technologies,
writers including Sarah Grand, appears also in anti-vivisection literature on the abuse of animals, such as Collins’s
Trangressing bodily boundaries is both suggestive and gendered; hence the allure of hypodermic needles. Braddon’s “Good Lady Ducayne” (1896) i
who saps the lives of hired young middle-class female companions by transfusing their blood into her veins in an attempt to regain youth and health. Bram Stoker’s
(1897) vests the vampire with such powers, and employs literal transfusion to combat that horror. It invokes a plethora of media an
books, magazines, newspapers, advertisements, shorthand, the telegraph, telephone, the phonograph, and the ty
in a struggle between west and east, modernity and
past distilled into a struggle over female bodies. The synecdochic relationship between women and technology figures large
provoked anxieties explored in texts such as George Gissing’s
(1897). The independence of the popular figure of the New Woman was epitomized by the mobility afforded by the safety bicycle, which became a reliable form of transportation from the late 1880s.
Although many Victorians embraced new technologies, a strong thread of critique runs from the Romantics through

, the aesthetic theories of John Ruskin, and the Arts and Crafts movement, all of which valued the organic over the mechanical. Poet and critic Matthew Arnold, the greatest Victorian influence on the formation of English studies, warned in
(1869) against “Faith in machinery” as the “besetting danger” of the age. We need to remember that
of the human in opposition to technology represents a particular ideological position, and that the implications of that position circulate not only in the
literature of the Victorian period but within the discipline of literary studies.
The Printed Image and the Transformation of Popular Culture
owered Knowledge: William Chambers and t
Moving Images: Nineteenth-Century Reading and Screen Practices
. Edinburgh Critical Studies in Victorian Culture. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press.
The Lives of Machines: The Industrial Imaginary in Victorian Literature and Culture
Telegraphic Realism: Victorian Fiction and Other Information Systems
ntellectual Property and the Victorian Novel
Victorian Technology: Invention, Innovation, and the Rise of the Machine
Imperial Media: Colonial Networks and Information Technologies in the British Literary Imagination, 1857–1918
Time and the Moment in Victorian Literature and Society
